questions.question_text: Under AREC Regulation 10.5, which name must appear in all real estate advertising?
questions.correct_answer: The firm name as recorded with AREC
distractors: The agent's personal legal name
The team name or DBA
The principal broker's home address
questions.explanation: The firm name as recorded with AREC must appear in all advertising. AREC's advertising guidance also recommends keeping the firm name at least as prominent as any licensee, team, or DBA name.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to advertise listings under a catchy trade name instead of the firm's licensed name. Under AREC advertising rules this is:
questions.correct_answer: Prohibited; ads must use the firm's licensed name
distractors: Permitted with the principal broker's consent
Permitted if the trade name is registered with the state
Permitted for digital advertising only
questions.explanation: AREC rules prohibit conducting real estate business under any name other than the name under which the principal broker is licensed, and the firm name must appear in every advertisement. AREC's best-practices guidance further recommends the firm name be at least as prominent as any team name.

questions.question_text: What citation can AREC issue for a non-compliant advertisement in Arkansas?
distractors: A citation of up to $100
A mandatory $1,000 fine
Immediate license suspension
questions.explanation: Advertising violations can draw a citation of up to $250, issued to the salesperson, the broker, or the supervising broker in lieu of formal proceedings. The $100 citation amount applies to education-deadline failures. Formal disciplinary proceedings can bring penalties of up to $1,000 per violation.

questions.question_text: A salesperson advertises a rental property they personally own. Must the ad include the firm name?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, even ads for licensee-owned property need it
distractors: No, personally owned property is exempt
Only if the firm will receive a commission
Only for sale listings, not rentals
questions.explanation: AREC rules require the firm name in all advertising, including advertising for property the licensee personally owns. Separately, the licensee must disclose their ownership interest in writing before a contract is signed.

questions.question_text: Under AREC's advertising guidance, who should develop and maintain the firm's advertising compliance policy?
distractors: Each individual agent
The Executive Broker only
AREC's compliance division
questions.explanation: AREC's advertising best-practices guidance recommends that Principal Brokers maintain an internal advertising policy, which also helps demonstrate the supervision AREC rules require of them. The binding rule is that the firm name appear in all advertising.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to purchase a listed property for personal use. What must they do?
questions.correct_answer: Provide full disclosure of their interest
distractors: Resign their license before purchasing
Obtain AREC approval for the purchase
Have another agent handle the entire deal
questions.explanation: When a licensee has a personal interest in a transaction, full disclosure is required. Self-dealing without disclosure is a violation of the license law.

questions.question_text: A salesperson buys an investment property listed by their own firm without disclosing their license status. This is:
distractors: Acceptable since they used a different agent
Only a problem if they got a reduced price
Permitted for investment properties
questions.explanation: Self-dealing without disclosure violates the license law regardless of whether the licensee uses a different agent within the firm. The personal interest must be disclosed.

questions.question_text: Self-dealing disclosure applies to which types of licensee interests?
questions.correct_answer: Purchases, sales, and any beneficial interest
distractors: Only direct purchases by the licensee
Only transactions over $100,000
Only when the licensee is the listing agent
questions.explanation: Self-dealing disclosure applies broadly — to purchases, sales, or any beneficial interest the licensee has in a transaction, including indirect ownership through entities.

questions.question_text: A broker lists a property owned by their spouse. What disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: The broker's personal interest must be disclosed
distractors: No disclosure needed for spousal property
Only disclosure to AREC, not the buyer
Disclosure only at the closing table
questions.explanation: A spouse's ownership represents a beneficial interest. The broker must disclose this personal connection to all parties in the transaction.

questions.question_text: Failure to disclose self-dealing in Arkansas can result in:
distractors: Only a verbal warning
A $100 advertising citation
No penalty if the transaction was fair
questions.explanation: Self-dealing without disclosure is a violation of the license law and can result in AREC disciplinary action including suspension, revocation, or monetary penalties.

questions.question_text: An agent refers clients to a moving company and receives a referral fee from it. What is required under Arkansas law?
questions.correct_answer: The referral fee arrangement must be disclosed
distractors: Referral fees from moving companies are prohibited
No disclosure is needed for non-brokerage services
Only the principal broker needs to be informed
questions.explanation: Licensees must be transparent about compensation from third-party service providers and disclose referral arrangements to affected clients. Note that for settlement-service providers (lenders, title companies, inspectors in financed deals), federal RESPA rules generally prohibit referral fees outright; disclosure does not make those lawful.

questions.question_text: Compensation arrangements between agents and service providers must be:
distractors: Kept confidential as business matters
Reported to AREC annually
Limited to 10% of the service cost
questions.explanation: Transparency about compensation arrangements with third-party service providers protects clients and avoids license-law problems such as undisclosed self-interest. Hidden fees can also violate federal RESPA rules when settlement services are involved.

questions.question_text: A listing agent steers all clients to a title company owned by the agent's family. No disclosure is made. This is:
questions.correct_answer: A violation requiring disclosure of the relationship
distractors: Acceptable business practice
Only a violation if the title company charges more
Permitted if the client doesn't ask
questions.explanation: Third-party service provider relationships must be disclosed. Steering clients to a family-owned company without disclosure violates the transparency requirement.

questions.question_text: Which of the following BEST describes Arkansas requirements for agent-service provider relationships?
questions.correct_answer: All financial relationships must be disclosed
distractors: Agents cannot receive any referral compensation
Only written referral agreements are regulated
Disclosure is voluntary for service providers
questions.explanation: Financial relationships between agents and service providers, including referral fees, ownership interests, and compensation, must be disclosed to clients. Undisclosed interests can constitute license-law violations, and settlement-service referral fees are separately restricted by federal RESPA.

questions.question_text: A lender offers an agent a gift card in exchange for referring buyers to the lender. Under federal RESPA rules, the agent should:
distractors: Accept it and disclose it to referred clients
Accept it only if under $25 in value
Accept it and report it to AREC
questions.explanation: A thing of value given for referring settlement-service business, such as mortgage lending, is a kickback prohibited by RESPA Section 8; disclosure does not cure it. Agents must decline referral compensation from lenders in transactions involving federally related mortgage loans.
